notes: Fixed the jump-to-latest race by making latest-page reducer updates atomic.
Replaced the previous timeout/scrollend workaround with a render-driven MessageList jump phase.
Extended the same stale-dispatch cancellation and render-driven behavior to jumpToMessage/highlighted-message flows.
Highlighted jumps now choose their entry edge from jump direction: older-page jumps start from bottom, newer-page jumps start from top.
Targeted MessageList and Channel regressions mock ChatView hooks because current suites do not mount ChatView providers by default.
Browser verification on the provided local thread reproduced a separate older-page prepend bug after several successful prepends.
Current prepend preservation in the scroll controller relies on scrollHeight deltas and appears too weak for repeated real-world prepends.
Attachment/media rows are a secondary risk factor for prepend drift, but browser inspection suggests they are not the primary root cause.
Anchor-based prepend restoration, center-anchor selection, and a bounded settle window improved early and mid prepend behavior but did not eliminate the later-page collapse.

Runtime instrumentation showed the later prepend failure was not caused by lost anchors or competing scroll writes; it was caused by preserving the old viewport even when pagination started from absolute top.

Older-page pagination now has two explicit modes: preserve the visible anchor when loading starts near the top, and stay pinned to the top of the newly loaded page when loading starts from absolute top.
Latest rebuilt browser verification on the provided thread kept scrollTop at 0 across the repeated 25 -> 225 older-page pagination sequence.
Split jumpToMessage into its own loading state so quoted-message jumps no longer reuse the older-page loading flag or prepend scroll-management path.
The exact benchat quoted-message browser repro still fails after the loading-state split and temporary previous-page suppression in MessageList.
Temporary browser-gated performance instrumentation was added for message-list scroll classification and anchor work.

A live browser profiling run on the rebuilt little-italy thread recorded classify-prepend at ~0.30ms and capture-anchor at ~0.20ms, which suggests prefix/suffix classification is not a meaningful bottleneck in the measured path.

A broader benchat profiling run while paginating the full channel confirmed that prefix/suffix classification stayed near 0-0.2ms, while captureAnchor grew into the 0.7-1.5ms range with spikes up to ~7.4ms as the rendered DOM grew.

browser: Fresh browser verification on http://localhost:5175/?mock_notifications=1&view=chat&channel=little-italy&thread=b472ce2e-ff3e-4fc0-8cef-8e75fe5002a4&theme=light reproduced repeated older-page prepends.
Observed later prepends where items increased and scrollHeight grew substantially while scrollTop changed only a few pixels, confirming prepend preservation drift in the browser.
After runtime instrumentation and the absolute-top pagination policy change, rebuilt browser verification kept scrollTop at 0 across repeated 25 -> 225 older-page prepends on the provided thread.

After splitting jumpToMessage loading from older-page pagination, rebuilt browser verification on http://localhost:5175/?mock_notifications=1&view=chat&channel=benchat&theme=light still stabilized at 50 messages with scrollTop ~264 and without the expected target id bc5efd05-eda7-4956-bc39-c3e6b4515fb0 in the DOM trace.

Performance profiling procedure on the rebuilt little-italy thread: enable window.__STREAM_MESSAGE_LIST_SCROLL_PERF__ = { enabled: true, entries: [] }, repeatedly scroll .str-chat__message-list to top to trigger older-page pagination, then aggregate entries by name from the browser console.

Measured live browser timings from that procedure: message-list-scroll:classify-prepend count 1 avg/max ~0.30ms, message-list-scroll:capture-anchor count 1 avg/max ~0.20ms, message-list-scroll:classify-append count 1 avg/max ~0ms.

A later benchat profiling run over full-channel pagination produced many entries and showed classify-prepend still around 0-0.2ms, classify-append effectively 0ms, apply-anchor mostly 0-0.3ms, and capture-anchor growing with rendered list size into the 0.7-1.5ms range with spikes up to ~7.4ms.
